title: Rustic X Farmhouse Dining Table
summary: A 2x6 plank-top farmhouse table on stacked curved-profile leg sets joined by an angled X stretcher — a genuinely different X-brace geometry from this catalog's other X-base tables.
description:
This table's base builds from stacked curved-edge boards rather than a simple straight-cut X or a pedestal, and its center stretcher uses two different compound angles rather than one repeated angle — a genuinely distinct X-brace geometry from the other X-based tables in this catalog. Build the tabletop first without its breadboard ends, using pocket holes, then **run a circular saw down the end edges with a clamped board as a guide** to true up all the boards to exactly the same length before the breadboard ends ever go on — trying to get eight individual 2x6 boards to a consistent length by measurement alone is far less reliable than trimming the assembled panel in one pass.

Once the first curved end board is cut correctly, **use it as a pattern for the rest** — the plan is explicit that this shape only needs to be gotten right once. Stack the curved boards in graduated rows onto the underside of the tabletop, hiding screws with countersinks where they'd otherwise show.

The center X is the base's real signature: **two angle cuts, 50° and 20° off square**, on legs that lean inward from both directions to cross in the middle — and the plan offers a genuine fallback if your saw doesn't cut a 50° miter: use two full-length 2x2s at a simple angle cut instead, overlapped and crossed, filling in gaps afterward if you want a fuller look. The stretcher underneath ties the two leg sets together and should be measured and cut to fit your actual leg spacing rather than to a fixed number, since 2x4 widths vary enough to shift that measurement slightly.

tools.note: Angle cuts for the X stretcher legs.
Truing up the tabletop board ends.

materials.name: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Use this measurement, not the diagram's.
Cut to fit against the trued-up tabletop.
Long point to long point, ends cut 30° off square, ends not parallel.
Long point to long point, ends cut 30° off square, ends not parallel.
Long point to long point, ends cut 30° off square, ends not parallel.
Measure and cut to fit your leg spacing.
Long point to short point, both ends cut parallel at 20° off square.
Longest point measurement, one end cut inward at 50°, other end inward at 20° off square.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the nine 2x6s, cut eight tabletop boards 83 1/2" long — one per board, since two will not fit on a 96" length — and get both 44" breadboard ends out of the ninth. From the 2x4s cut three long curved leg boards at 38", four mid at 35 1/2", and four short at 33", all measured long point to long point with both ends cut 30 degrees off square and NOT parallel; then eight leg feet at 20 1/4" and the stretcher at 64", which you will trim to your actual leg spacing later. Rip two more 2x4s down to 1 1/2" square for the X center support at 68 5/8" and the two X arms at 34" — the materials list has no 2x2 line, so those come out of 2x4 stock. Cut the two 36" foot trim pieces from the 1x4. All nine 2x6s and all nine 2x4s are consumed with very little to spare, so cut the longest parts out of each board first and take the short pieces from what is left.

steps.title: Build the tabletop
steps.body: Lay the eight 83 1/2" boards face down edge to edge — no breadboard ends yet — and join them with 1 1/2" pocket holes and 2 1/2" pocket hole screws, staggering the holes so they do not all crowd the same line. Eight 5 1/2" boards give a 44" wide top, which is a useful check: that is exactly the breadboard-end length in the cut list. Then clamp a straight board across each end as a guide and run the circular saw down both end edges, truing all eight boards to one length in a single pass — getting eight individual 2x6s to a consistent length by measurement alone is far less reliable. For a smoother top, consider tongue and groove boards.

steps.title: Attach the breadboard ends
steps.body: Measure the trued-up top's actual width — it should land close to 44" — and cut the two breadboard ends to that measured figure rather than to the cut-list number, since the trimming pass just changed it. Attach one to each end with pocket holes and 2 1/2" screws driven from underneath, keeping the top surfaces flush; a breadboard end sitting proud is the first thing a hand finds on a dining table. Clamp across the joint while you drive the screws so nothing creeps.

steps.title: Cut and stack the curved leg boards
steps.body: Cut the pattern for the curved edge boards, getting one end right, then use it as a pattern for the rest. Attach the three longest curved boards to the underside of the tabletop, then stack the next row on top, countersinking screws where they'd otherwise show.

steps.title: Add the second leg base and feet
steps.body: Add the second base row to the leg set, then attach the feet and foot trim.

steps.title: Attach the leg sets and stretcher
steps.body: Attach the leg sets to the underside of the tabletop. Measure and cut the stretcher to fit the actual leg spacing, then attach with pocket holes underneath.

steps.title: Build the center X
steps.body: Cut the X center support at 20° parallel ends, and the two X arms with a 50° cut on one end and a 20° cut on the other. If your saw can't cut 50°, substitute two full-length 2x2s overlapped at a simple angle cut instead. Attach the X assembly to the stretcher and leg sets.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand all surfaces and apply primer and paint, or stain and poly.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Farmhouse dining table with stacked curved-profile X-brace legs
